turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court in Case 24-249, A.J.T. v. Osseo Area Schools. Petitioner is a teenage girl with the rare form of epilepsy that severely limits her physical and cognitive functioning. We refer to her by the initials A.J.T. to protect her anonymity. A.J.T. suffers from seizures that are so frequent in the mornings that she cannot attend school before noon, though she is alert and able to learn from noon until 6:00 p.m. For the first few years of her schooling, school officials accommodated A.J.T.'s condition by permitting her to avoid morning activities and instead receive afternoon and evening instruction. But when A.J.T.'s family moved to another State, her new school denied her parents' repeated requests to provide evening instruction. So, for three years, A.J.T. received only four hours of instruction daily compared to the typical six-and-a-half-hour school day. After even further cuts to A.J.T.'s school day were proposed, her parents filed a complaint under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act, the IDEA seeking compensatory education and evening instruction. Federal courts ruled in favor of A.J.T. and her parents. A.J.T. and her parents then sued under the Americans with Disabilities Act, the ADA, and the Rehabilitation Act. Those statutes are not limited to education, but provide general protection against discrimination on the basis of disability. They could provide a permanent injunction, reimbursement for certain costs and compensatory damages. But A.J.T. and her parents lost this time. For most cases seeking relief for disability discrimination under the ADA and Rehabilitation Act, you can obtain injunctive relief by proving a statutory violation. You can get monetary damages by showing the violation was intentional, but showing deliberate indifference is enough to meet that standard. The Court of Appeals here, however, like several others around the country, held that a different, more rigorous standard applied when the disability discrimination claim, like A.J.T.'s is about educational opportunities. In such a case, you have to show "bad faith or gross misjudgment to get relief." Even though the same statutory language is at issue. Now, why the much heavier burden for disability claims at school? Well, some courts reasoned that the education context was different because the IDEA applied there as well as the ADA and the Rehabilitation Act. Those courts thought that requiring the harder showing of bad faith or gross misjudgment was necessary to "harmonize" the ADA and the Rehabilitation Act with the IDEA and to reflect the proper balance between disabled children's rights, state officials' responsibilities, and courts' competence in technical fields. Today we hold that, however well-intentioned, that reasoning is wrong. Congress applied the same statutory language to disability discrimination under the ADA and Rehabilitation Act, regardless of whether the discrimination claim arises from school, work, public facilities, whatever. The laws say they apply to any person, to any person aggrieved, and to any qualified individual, without distinction based on where the discrimination occurred, at school or outside the school. Having different rules to harmonize claims under the same statute in different places is plainly for Congress to decide, not the courts. And Congress has made clear that nothing in the IDEA restricts or limits the ability of school children to vindicate their independent ADA and Rehabilitation Act rights. Children with severe disabilities and their parents face daunting challenges. We hold that having to satisfy a more stringent standard of proof than other plaintiffs to establish discrimination under the ADA and the Rehabilitation Act is not one of those challenges. The judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit is vacated and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The opinion of the court is unanimous. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion in which Justice Kavanaugh joined. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurring opinion in which Justice Jackson joined.
